name: Hermes Agent
summary: The runtime itself. Persistent memory, auto-generated skills, 14 messaging platforms, 6 execution backends, cron, gateway, profiles. Everything else on this list plugs into or wraps this.
best: primary agent runtime
terminal and gateway deployments
homelab and self-hosted setups
notFor: quick one-shot tasks where a simple script would do
environments where shell execution is a hard no
verified: 2026-06-16: running daily as primary agent. Two concurrent instances (Callisto + Janus) on the same host without conflict.
recommendation: Baseline. Treat configs, memory, gateway tokens, and profile state as operationally sensitive surfaces. Run under a dedicated service account, not root.

name: Hermes Native Memory
summary: Built-in memory tooling: the memory store (4000/2500 char limits), session DB with FTS5 full-text search, and the session_search tool that reaches back through all indexed history. No install, no network dependency.
best: profile memory and user preferences
cross-session recall
local-first environments with no cloud dependency
notFor: relational or graph-style fact storage
querying facts across multiple entities simultaneously
verified: 2026-06-16: memory store, session_search, and FTS5 recall in daily use across two profiles.
recommendation: Default to this first. The session DB is surprisingly capable for recall. Only reach for external memory when you've actually hit a ceiling here.

name: Honcho
summary: Dialectic memory layer — builds a persistent peer model (who the user is, what they want, how they communicate) using LLM reasoning over observed conversation. Surfaces as honcho_profile, honcho_search, honcho_reasoning, and honcho_conclude tools.
best: long-term user modeling
persona-aware responses that improve over time
sessions where knowing the person matters more than knowing the facts
notFor: factual key-value storage (use fact_store for that)
low-latency recall (reasoning calls are not cheap)
environments where you want zero external API calls
verified: 2026-06-16: running in production. honcho_profile and honcho_search in daily use. dialectic reasoning works but costs more than a straight lookup — use reasoning_level: minimal for factual queries.
recommendation: Worth it for agents with long-running relationships with users. The peer model actually compounds — it gets more useful over time. Call honcho_search before honcho_reasoning; it's cheaper and often sufficient.

name: Holographic SQLite Fact Store
summary: Local Hermes plugin: SQLite-backed fact store with entity resolution, trust scoring, and algebraic queries (probe, reason across entities, contradict for hygiene). Fills the gap native memory has for relational/graph-style recall.
best: relational facts about people and projects
multi-entity reasoning (what connects X and Y?)
contradiction detection across memory over time
notFor: narrative or behavioral memory (Honcho does that better)
high-frequency writes (trust scoring adds overhead)
verified: 2026-06-16: 13 facts stored, entity probe and reason queries working. Located at /opt/hermes/plugins/memory/holographic/.
recommendation: Use alongside native memory and Honcho, not instead of either. The three-layer stack (native + Honcho + fact_store) covers different shapes of memory. Probe entities before answering user questions about people or projects.

name: mem0
summary: Official Hermes memory provider. Universal memory layer with semantic search, personalization, and managed cloud or self-hosted options. 58k+ stars, significant momentum.
best: cloud-managed memory with no self-hosting overhead
teams sharing memory across multiple agents
notFor: strict local-only environments
operators who want to inspect and own their memory store directly
verified: Not installed. Reviewed architecture and docs. Cloud-first despite self-hosted option.
recommendation: Plausible if you're comfortable with managed infrastructure. For local-first operators, the native + Honcho + fact_store stack covers the same ground without phoning home.

name: GBrain
summary: Opinionated memory and synthesis layer with knowledge graph, cited answers, and gap analysis. Designed for deep project knowledge accumulation.
best: knowledge graph memory for complex projects
cited synthesis with source tracing
notFor: lightweight personal memory
operators who want minimal dependencies
verified: Reviewed, not installed. Experimental status in upstream.
recommendation: Sandbox first. Interesting for project-scale knowledge synthesis. Not a drop-in for personal memory until someone field-tests the install story.

name: Mnemosyne
summary: Zero-dependency local memory system. No network, no database server, no config overhead.
best: minimal offline memory experiments
notFor: production use until someone verifies install + recall quality
verified: Reviewed, not installed. Low activity on the repo.
recommendation: Low blast radius makes it worth a test if you want a zero-dep alternative. Needs a field report before recommending it.

name: Systematic Debugging Skill
summary: Root-cause-first debugging workflow: understand the bug, reproduce it, isolate the cause, fix, verify. Enforces the discipline of not patching before diagnosing.
best: bugs with unclear cause
regressions where the fix isn't obvious
any situation where the temptation is to patch first and understand later
notFor: typos and obvious one-line fixes
verified: 2026-06-16: in regular use. The 'understand before fixing' constraint alone pays for itself.
recommendation: Load this skill whenever a bug isn't obviously a typo. The four-phase structure (understand, reproduce, isolate, fix) is the right discipline, and having it encoded means the agent doesn't skip steps under pressure.

name: Token Compression Skill
summary: Compresses behavioral and procedural skill files using a compact DSL. Reduces token cost for large skill/SOUL files while keeping operational commands intact.
best: large skill files
SOUL and persona headers
anything behavioral that doesn't need exact syntax preserved
notFor: skills with precise CLI commands or copy-paste code blocks — compression can corrupt exact syntax
verified: 2026-06-16: used on 82 skills. Works well on behavioral content. Confirmed risky on exact command sequences.
recommendation: Use surgically. Good for prose-heavy behavioral content; dangerous on anything where the exact string matters. Always diff before committing compressed skills to production.

name: Hermes Skill Factory
summary: Meta-skill that watches your workflows and automatically generates reusable skills from patterns it observes. Self-improving skill corpus.
best: operators who run repetitive workflows and want auto-captured skills
notFor: production environments where you want full control over what's in your skill corpus
verified: Not installed. Concept is interesting but auto-generated skills can accumulate quietly.
recommendation: Interesting idea. Concern is quality control — auto-generated skills mixed into a working corpus can introduce silent regressions. Would want a review gate before any auto-merged skill.

name: Avoid AI Writing
summary: Audits and rewrites content to eliminate detectable AI writing patterns. Strips the tell-tale hedges, hollow affirmations, and cadence that marks LLM output.
best: content destined for public audiences
anywhere voice consistency matters
notFor: internal notes and technical docs where AI-ness doesn't matter
recommendation: Worth adding if you generate content for human audiences. The problem it solves is real and the approach (pattern audit + rewrite) is sound.

name: Service Account Deployment
summary: Run Hermes gateway and runtime under a dedicated non-root service account with systemd supervision. Least-privilege posture for production deployments.
best: VPS and homelab production deployments
any gateway-exposed instance
notFor: quick local dev where you don't care about privilege isolation
verified: 2026-06-16: systemd service, file ownership, and env-file pattern verified in production.
recommendation: Default production posture. Inspect file ownership and env-file permissions before changing a running agent — they drift easily when you're installing as a different user than the service account.

name: Docker + s6-overlay Container
summary: Hermes running inside Docker with s6-overlay for process supervision. Multiple services (agent, gateway, Discord, Telegram) managed as a supervised tree. Persistent state via mounted volumes.
best: running multiple concurrent Hermes instances on one host
environments where container isolation is preferred
homelab setups with Proxmox or similar
notFor: simple single-instance setups where systemd is sufficient
verified: 2026-06-16: two instances (Callisto, Janus) running concurrently on containership via this pattern. s6 supervision handles restarts cleanly.
recommendation: Solid pattern once you understand s6's supervision tree model. The main gotcha: s6 services use their own init sequence — don't mix shell backgrounding tricks with s6 managed processes. See hermes-s6-container-supervision skill.

name: Agent-to-Agent (A2A) Protocol
summary: Custom coordination layer for two concurrent Hermes instances (Callisto and Janus) sharing a Discord server. Shared JSONL state file, busy-flag mutex, and channel-based message routing. No central orchestrator.
best: two-agent setups where instances need to coordinate without stepping on each other
homelab multi-agent where both instances share a messaging surface
notFor: fleet-scale orchestration (this is a two-agent pattern, not a framework)
environments without persistent shared filesystem
verified: 2026-06-16: Callisto + Janus operating in parallel via this pattern. Busy-flag collision avoidance working. Shared state via /opt/data/a2a-state.jsonl.
recommendation: Works well for a two-agent homelab setup. The busy-flag is essential — without it the instances will talk over each other on shared channels. Check the a2a-protocol skill before implementing.

name: Claude Code
summary: Autonomous coding CLI. Hermes can delegate feature work, PRs, and large refactors to it as a subagent. High capability, high blast radius.
best: large refactors where you want an autonomous coding pass
PR review and inline comment workflows
delegated coding tasks with well-defined scope
notFor: anything touching production secrets or live infrastructure without a review gate
tasks where you haven't defined the scope clearly first
verified: 2026-06-16: skill orchestration tested. Delegation via claude-code skill works. External CLI auth required separately.
recommendation: Powerful but earn the trust slowly. Use worktrees to isolate changes, set max-turns, define scope explicitly. Always diff before merging. The claude-code skill in Hermes encodes the safe delegation pattern — load it.

name: Hermes WebUI (nesquena)
summary: Web and mobile interface for Hermes Agent. Most popular WebUI in the ecosystem. Active development, 14k+ stars.
best: remote access to Hermes from phone or browser
users who aren't comfortable with a terminal
notFor: operators who are fine in terminal and don't need a web layer
verified: Not installed. High stars and active development are real signals. Haven't verified auth handling or secret exposure surface.
recommendation: Worth evaluating if you need non-terminal access. Any web UI sitting in front of an agent deserves a close look at auth and how it handles the gateway token. Don't expose it to the internet without auth.

name: Hermes Workspace (outsourc-e)
summary: Native web workspace with chat, terminal, memory browser, skills manager, and inspector panel. More operator-focused than a simple chat UI.
best: operators who want a dashboard view into memory and skills alongside chat
anyone who wants to inspect session state without using the terminal
notFor: operators comfortable working in the CLI who don't need a dashboard layer
verified: Not installed. The memory browser and skills manager panels are the interesting differentiator over simpler WebUIs.
recommendation: More interesting than a plain chat wrapper because of the inspector panel. Would want to verify how deeply it integrates vs. just proxying the gateway before committing.

name: Hermes Self-Evolution
summary: Official NousResearch project: evolutionary self-improvement via DSPy + GEPA. Optimizes skills, prompts, and code by running evaluation loops and keeping what scores better.
best: research and experimental self-improvement setups
operators who want to automate skill quality improvement at scale
notFor: production instances where you want stable, predictable behavior
operators who haven't already built a solid skill corpus manually
verified: Not installed. Official repo, 4k+ stars. Conceptually powerful. Risky to run against a corpus you depend on without a full backup and rollback path.
recommendation: Fascinating project but treat it like surgery on a running system. Don't run it on your primary profile without a way to roll back. Worth watching as it matures.
